title: China debrief: Ferrari's early lead looks more real with every lap
meta: Published: 17 March 2026 | F1CC News Race Desk
caption: Lead image: Ferrari celebrates after Shanghai.
body:
Two races into 2026, the most important thing about Ferrari's start is not that Eetu Väisänen has won again. It is that the team has now won in two radically different ways. Melbourne was a weather-ravaged test of survival and opportunism. Shanghai was a cold, precise strategic puzzle that demanded patience, perfect pit-wall calls, and the nerve to stick with a contrarian tyre choice. Ferrari solved both without flinching, and that is what should worry the rest of the paddock most.

This is not a flash of form. Väisänen finished 17th with 46 points in 2024, climbed to seventh with 165 in 2025, and now leads a team that has finally alchemised years of development into something that looks durable. The championship table is beginning to resemble less a hot streak and more a statement of intent.

The Red Bull Mistake That Handed Ferrari Control

If Shanghai provided Ferrari with a strategic masterclass, it also exposed a critical misjudgement in the Red Bull pit wall. Jason Roy started from pole and led the opening laps comfortably. When the safety car was deployed for the first-lap carnage, Red Bull made the defining call of the afternoon: they brought Roy in for hard tyres, the only driver other than Väisänen to switch to that compound so early. This was not a panicked reaction to a sticky wheel gun. It was a calculated gamble, born directly from seeing the Ferrari on hards from the start.

Red Bull’s logic was easy to follow. They saw Väisänen’s contrarian strategy and concluded that the hard tyre was the race-winner. With a safety car compressing the field, they believed they could sacrifice track position, bolt on the same compound, and use Roy’s superior starting position to hunt Väisänen down later. In effect, they tried to copy Ferrari’s homework — and got it badly wrong.

What Red Bull underestimated was that Ferrari had built their entire race around that tyre choice. Väisänen was fuelled and set up to maximise a long first stint, and the safety car simply accelerated a plan already in motion. Roy, by contrast, had been running softs, had not banked any stint length, and his stop dropped him into traffic on a compound he had not prepared for. The strategic initiative handed itself to Maranello on a plate, and Red Bull spent the rest of the afternoon fighting a race they had already lost. It was an error, plain and simple — a misread of the strategic landscape that a team of Red Bull’s calibre should not be making.

No Lucky Breaks Required

The most sobering detail for the rest of the grid is that Ferrari have not needed a single slice of fortune. Melbourne’s rain might have scrambled the order, but Väisänen’s victory there was built on the same pillars as Shanghai: strong qualifying when it mattered, intelligent tyre management, and a pit wall that read the race better than anyone else. In dry conditions and wet, in sprints and feature races, the scarlet cars have simply looked the most organised operation on the grid. Those are the traits that decide championships, not individual grands prix.

Shanghai also reminded everyone that the fight behind Ferrari remains wide open. Quarter Kevu’s second-place finish gave VCARB another serious result and kept him 30 points adrift in the standings — a gap that is already larger than the one between himself and fourteenth-placed Riley Swansson. Jason Roy’s podium, salvaged from a strategic blunder, keeps him only eighth in the championship on 15 points, level with the damaged Alpine of Michiil MaraDöner. Zhao Lei’s home-race fourth moved him to third with 19 points, but the 38-point chasm to the leader is the real story. Wesley Vandesteene, after a brilliant P2 in Melbourne, followed it with a first-lap lock-up and retirement — exactly the kind of yo-yoing form that cannot sustain a title challenge. Eren Aygen and Robin Keisner share fifth on 16 points, the latter’s last-to-fifth heroics marking him as the grid’s most exciting disruptor.

The Comfort Zone

If there is still a question hanging over the early season, it is whether anyone can force Ferrari to race outside their comfort zone. Can Red Bull translate one-lap pace into sustained pressure without tripping over their own strategy? Can VCARB turn Kevu’s consistency into a genuine threat? Can Mercedes stop haemorrhaging points through errors and penalties? Right now, the answer to all of these is a firm no. And after Shanghai, that answer feels even firmer than it did after Melbourne.

Ferrari are not unbeatable — no team is — but they have built a lead that allows them to lose a round and still lead comfortably. That is the luxury of a team that has started the season not just fast, but smart. The paddock heads to Suzuka with upgrades promised across the grid, but the mountain everyone must now climb has already grown tall. Two races in, the 2026 title is Ferrari’s to lose.
